scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel framework that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds. LWGAN combines Wasserstein Auto-Encoders (WAE) and Wasserstein GANs (WGAN) to improve generative modeling and representation learning by identifying the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold. It theoretically proves the existence of an encoder and generator network where the learned encoding distribution's intrinsic dimension matches the data manifold's dimension and establishes the consistency of the estimated intrinsic dimension and provides an upper bound on the generalization error.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN uses a modified latent distribution, specifically a generalized normal distribution N(0, A) where A is a diagonal matrix with 0 or 1 entries, to characterize the intrinsic dimension of the latent space. It combines a deterministic encoder Q: X -> Z to learn an informative prior distribution PZ ~ N(0, A) with a generator G: Z -> X to generate realistic images. The framework minimizes the 1-Wasserstein distance between the real and generated data distributions, regularized by a rank penalty on matrix A.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework was validated using synthetic datasets (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image datasets (MNIST, CelebA). The performance was evaluated based on the accuracy of intrinsic dimension detection, visual quality of generated samples, and quantitative metrics like Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error. Baselines included WGAN, WAE, and CycleGAN.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper assumes the existence of a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The generator G is assumed to be continuous. The 1-Lipschitz constraint on the critic f is enforced by the gradient penalty technique.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general scenario where the generator G is stochastic by adding an extra noise vector to its input. It also suggests incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules such as BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images along with the estimation of the intrinsic dimension. Applying LWGAN for structural estimation in economics is also proposed.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
The Stronger the Diffusion Model, the Easier the Backdoor: Data
  Poisoning to Induce Copyright Breaches Without Adjusting Finetuning Pipeline
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
The commercialization of text-to-image diffusion models (DMs) brings forth
potential copyright concerns. Despite numerous attempts to protect DMs from
copyright issues, the vulnerabilities of these solutions are underexplored. In
this study, we formalized the Copyright Infringement Attack on generative AI
models and proposed a backdoor attack method, SilentBadDiffusion, to induce
copyright infringement without requiring access to or control over training
processes. Our method strategically embeds connections between pieces of
copyrighted information and text references in poisoning data while carefully
dispersing that information, making the poisoning data inconspicuous when
integrated into a clean dataset. Our experiments show the stealth and efficacy
of the poisoning data. When given specific text prompts, DMs trained with a
poisoning ratio of 0.20% can produce copyrighted images. Additionally, the
results reveal that the more sophisticated the DMs are, the easier the success
of the attack becomes. These findings underline potential pitfalls in the
prevailing copyright protection strategies and underscore the necessity for
increased scrutiny to prevent the misuse of DMs.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper formalizes the Copyright Infringement Attack on generative AI models and introduces a backdoor attack method called SilentBadDiffusion. This method induces copyright infringement in text-to-image diffusion models by strategically embedding connections between copyrighted information and text references in poisoning data, without requiring access to the training or fine-tuning processes. The research demonstrates the stealth and efficacy of the poisoning data, showing that more sophisticated DMs are more vulnerable to this attack.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: SilentBadDiffusion involves semantically dissecting a copyrighted image into nuanced elements and incorporating them into multiple images via inpainting, along with descriptive text captions. A multi-modal large language model (MLLM) is used to identify salient parts of a target image and describe them with phrases. A semantic segmentation model then identifies and segments the visual element corresponding to the phrase. Poisoning images are generated by inpainting around the isolated visual element, guided by generated image captions.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted on Stable Diffusion models (v1.1 to v1.5) using the Pokemon BLIP Captions dataset and a subset of the LAION Aesthetics v2 6.5+ dataset, as well as the COYO-700m dataset. The attack's effectiveness was evaluated based on the Copyright Infringement Rate (CIR) and the First-Attack Epoch (FAE). Self Supervised Copy Detection (SSCD) was used to measure the substantial similarity between generated images and copyrighted images.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach assumes that copyright images are decomposable. The study also acknowledges ethical and legal concerns related to the use of datasets like LAION and the Pokemon dataset, which were later found to contain problematic content. The effectiveness of the attack may diminish with significantly reduced portions of poisoning data.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on a broader range of targets, including non-decomposable images. Leveraging optimization techniques to subtly integrate copyright information by updating pixel-wise values of training data is another promising direction. Exploring style-attack and multiple-backdoors attack scenarios are also suggested for future exploration.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces a novel framework for training diffusion models using corrupted data, enabling exact sampling from the uncorrupted distribution. Key contributions include a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function to extend sampling below the observed data noise. The paper also provides evidence of memorization in diffusion models and demonstrates the framework's ability to mitigate this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL with noisy samples.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The method employs a double application of Tweedie's formula to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above that of the training data. A consistency loss function is used to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels below that of the training data, ensuring the model remains consistent during the reverse diffusion process.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) finetuned on FFHQ and a subset of LAION. The models were trained with varying levels of noise corruption. Evaluation metrics included FID score, MSE, and a novel method for detecting training data replication based on the similarity of reconstructed highly corrupted images to the training dataset.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not address training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases the training time. The proposed Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work on very limited datasets (< 100 samples).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include solving the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data while ensuring sampling from the uncorrupted distribution, reducing training time with consistency, and testing the proposed Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective on more diverse and comprehensive datasets.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title: Accelerating Parallel Sampling of Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for image
generation. However, sampling from diffusion models is usually time-consuming
due to the inherent autoregressive nature of their sampling process. In this
work, we propose a novel approach that accelerates the sampling of diffusion
models by parallelizing the autoregressive process. Specifically, we
reformulate the sampling process as solving a system of triangular nonlinear
equations through fixed-point iteration. With this innovative formulation, we
explore several systematic techniques to further reduce the iteration steps
required by the solving process. Applying these techniques, we introduce
ParaTAA, a universal and training-free parallel sampling algorithm that can
leverage extra computational and memory resources to increase the sampling
speed. Our experiments demonstrate that ParaTAA can decrease the inference
steps required by common sequential sampling algorithms such as DDIM and DDPM
by a factor of 4$\sim$14 times. Notably, when applying ParaTAA with 100 steps
DDIM for Stable Diffusion, a widely-used text-to-image diffusion model, it can
produce the same images as the sequential sampling in only 7 inference steps.
The code is available at https://github.com/TZW1998/ParaTAA-Diffusion.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces ParaTAA, a novel training-free parallel sampling algorithm for diffusion models that accelerates the sampling process by reformulating it as solving a system of triangular nonlinear equations through fixed-point iteration. Key contributions include techniques to reduce iteration steps and a tailored Anderson Acceleration method.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The approach involves reformulating the diffusion sampling process as solving triangular nonlinear equations using fixed-point iteration. It incorporates techniques such as equivalent transformation of nonlinear equations, a new variant of Anderson Acceleration tailored for triangular nonlinear equations (TAA), early stopping based on perceptual criteria, and initialization with solutions from similar equations.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using DiT models trained on the ImageNet dataset (256x256 resolution) and Stable Diffusion v1.5 (512x512 resolution). The method was evaluated on DDIM (25, 50, 100 steps) and DDPM (100 steps) sampling algorithms. Image quality was assessed using FID and Inception Score for DiT and CLIP Score for Stable Diffusion.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method currently requires multiple GPUs for large models like Stable Diffusion to achieve significant wall-clock time speedup. Also, predicting the optimal order 'k' for the nonlinear equations from a theoretical standpoint is not feasible, requiring empirical selection.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include broadening the application of the method to other autoregressive processes such as video generative models and further exploration of the theoretical aspects of the proposed Triangular Anderson Acceleration (TAA) technique. Additionally, improvements in GPU technology and parallel computing infrastructures could enhance the applicability of ParaTAA.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces a novel framework for training diffusion models using only corrupted samples, enabling exact sampling from the uncorrupted distribution. Key contributions include a computationally efficient method based on a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function for extending sampling to noise levels below the observed data noise. The paper also provides evidence of memorization in diffusion models and demonstrates the framework's ability to reduce memorization while maintaining performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The method uses a double application of Tweedie's formula to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above that of the training data. A consistency loss function is then employed to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels below that of the training data, ensuring consistency in the diffusion process. Stochastic Differential Equations are used for sampling.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework is evaluated by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL on corrupted data, specifically the FFHQ dataset, at 1024x1024 resolution, and a subset of LAION. The performance is measured using FID score, MSE reconstruction error, and a novel attack to detect training data replication by solving inverse problems at high corruption levels. DINOv2 latent space is used to measure the similarity between generated and training images.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not address training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. The Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective may not work well on very limited datasets.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include solving the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data, reducing the training time associated with consistency training, and exploring the performance of the Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective on limited datasets.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Sequential Neural Score Estimation: Likelihood-Free Inference with
  Conditional Score Based Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce Sequential Neural Posterior Score Estimation (SNPSE), a
score-based method for Bayesian inference in simulator-based models. Our
method, inspired by the remarkable success of score-based methods in generative
modelling, leverages conditional score-based diffusion models to generate
samples from the posterior distribution of interest. The model is trained using
an objective function which directly estimates the score of the posterior. We
embed the model into a sequential training procedure, which guides simulations
using the current approximation of the posterior at the observation of
interest, thereby reducing the simulation cost. We also introduce several
alternative sequential approaches, and discuss their relative merits. We then
validate our method, as well as its amortised, non-sequential, variant on
several numerical examples, demonstrating comparable or superior performance to
existing state-of-the-art methods such as Sequential Neural Posterior
Estimation (SNPE).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Sequential Neural Posterior Score Estimation (SNPSE), a novel score-based method for Bayesian inference in simulator-based models. It leverages conditional score-based diffusion models to sample from the posterior distribution by directly estimating the score of the posterior. The method includes a sequential training procedure to reduce simulation costs and demonstrates comparable or superior performance to existing state-of-the-art methods like SNPE.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology utilizes conditional score-based diffusion models. A time-varying score network is trained to approximate the score of the perturbed posterior using score matching techniques. The model is embedded in a sequential training procedure, guiding simulations using the current posterior approximation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is validated on several numerical examples and benchmark SBI problems, including SLCP, Lotka-Volterra, and a real-world neuroscience problem involving the pyloric network of the crab Cancer Borealis. The performance is evaluated using the classification-based two-sample test (C2ST) score and comparison against existing SBI methods.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The primary limitation is the computational cost associated with computing the highest probability region (HPR) of the approximate posterior, which involves evaluating the posterior density over many samples. TSNPSE, which uses a CNF, requires multiple forward passes for sampling, and multiple gradients of forward passes for density evaluation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include developing sequential variants of Flow Matching Posterior Estimation (FMPE) and investigating effective network designs for SBI problems, exploring specialized architectures used in diffusion models for other modalities.

selected_base_paper_info.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
selected_base_paper_info.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
selected_base_paper_info.main_contributions: The paper introduces the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel framework that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds. LWGAN combines Wasserstein Auto-Encoders (WAE) and Wasserstein GANs (WGAN) to improve generative modeling and representation learning by identifying the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold. It theoretically proves the existence of an encoder and generator network where the learned encoding distribution's intrinsic dimension matches the data manifold's dimension and establishes the consistency of the estimated intrinsic dimension and provides an upper bound on the generalization error.
selected_base_paper_info.methodology: LWGAN uses a modified latent distribution, specifically a generalized normal distribution N(0, A) where A is a diagonal matrix with 0 or 1 entries, to characterize the intrinsic dimension of the latent space. It combines a deterministic encoder Q: X -> Z to learn an informative prior distribution PZ ~ N(0, A) with a generator G: Z -> X to generate realistic images. The framework minimizes the 1-Wasserstein distance between the real and generated data distributions, regularized by a rank penalty on matrix A.
selected_base_paper_info.experimental_setup: The framework was validated using synthetic datasets (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image datasets (MNIST, CelebA). The performance was evaluated based on the accuracy of intrinsic dimension detection, visual quality of generated samples, and quantitative metrics like Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error. Baselines included WGAN, WAE, and CycleGAN.
selected_base_paper_info.limitations: The paper assumes the existence of a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The generator G is assumed to be continuous. The 1-Lipschitz constraint on the critic f is enforced by the gradient penalty technique.
selected_base_paper_info.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general scenario where the generator G is stochastic by adding an extra noise vector to its input. It also suggests incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules such as BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images along with the estimation of the intrinsic dimension. Applying LWGAN for structural estimation in economics is also proposed.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: BTLM-3B-8K: 7B Parameter Performance in a 3B Parameter Model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce the Bittensor Language Model, called "BTLM-3B-8K", a new
state-of-the-art 3 billion parameter open-source language model. BTLM-3B-8K was
trained on 627B tokens from the SlimPajama dataset with a mixture of 2,048 and
8,192 context lengths. BTLM-3B-8K outperforms all existing 3B parameter models
by 2-5.5% across downstream tasks. BTLM-3B-8K is even competitive with some 7B
parameter models. Additionally, BTLM-3B-8K provides excellent long context
performance, outperforming MPT-7B-8K and XGen-7B-8K on tasks up to 8,192
context length. We trained the model on a cleaned and deduplicated SlimPajama
dataset; aggressively tuned the \textmu P hyperparameters and schedule; used
ALiBi position embeddings; and adopted the SwiGLU nonlinearity.
  On Hugging Face, the most popular models have 7B parameters, indicating that
users prefer the quality-size ratio of 7B models. Compacting the 7B parameter
model to one with 3B parameters, with little performance impact, is an
important milestone. BTLM-3B-8K needs only 3GB of memory with 4-bit precision
and takes 2.5x less inference compute than 7B models, helping to open up access
to a powerful language model on mobile and edge devices. BTLM-3B-8K is
available under an Apache 2.0 license on Hugging Face:
https://huggingface.co/cerebras/btlm-3b-8k-base.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces BTLM-3B-8K, a new 3 billion parameter open-source language model that achieves state-of-the-art performance among 3B parameter models and is competitive with some 7B parameter models, especially in long context tasks. The key contributions include the training procedure, model evaluation across various benchmarks, training improvement ablations, and the release of the BTLM-3B-8K weights and SlimPajama dataset.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: BTLM-3B-8K is an autoregressive transformer decoder model based on the GPT-3 architecture with fully dense attention. It incorporates SwiGLU nonlinearity, ALiBi position embeddings, and maximal update parameterization (µP). The model was trained in two phases with different sequence lengths, using the AdamW optimizer and a linear warmup and decay learning rate schedule.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The model was trained on the SlimPajama dataset, a cleaned and deduplicated version of RedPajama, containing 627B tokens. It was evaluated on 22 benchmarks, including common sense reasoning, world knowledge, reading comprehension, MMLU, code generation, and long context tasks. The evaluation used zero-shot and few-shot tasks from the EleutherAI evaluation harness framework. Long context performance was evaluated on QMSum, GovReports, and LongEval tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The model exhibits biases, toxicity, and truthfulness characteristics similar to existing models. ALiBi alone does not provide competitive extrapolation capability and the model learns to “overfit” to the current context length. BTLM-3B-8K underperforms every 7B model in average world knowledge accuracy. The model was only trained and evaluated following the approaches described in the paper.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests using variable context length training schedules to improve extrapolation performance. It recommends exploring harm mitigation strategies in deployment contexts and careful dataset curation techniques to reduce model harmfulness. Further research could focus on instruction-following or chat-based use cases. Additional safety-related testing and mitigations should be applied before using the model in production downstream applications.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Purify++, an improved diffusion purification algorithm for defending against adversarial attacks. It addresses limitations in previous diffusion purification methods by using an improved diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ utilizes an improved forward diffusion process based on Variance Exploding (VE) diffusion, efficient purification simulation using Heun's method, and optimal control of randomness by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics. The algorithm integrates the EDM diffusion model, Heun's simulation, and a randomness control mechanism.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The algorithm was evaluated on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets using various adversarial attacks, including black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive attacks (BPDA+EOT). Performance was measured using standard and robust accuracy, comparing against existing adversarial defense methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The evaluation of the adaptive attack (BPDA+EOT) was performed on a subset of the CIFAR10 test set due to high computational costs. The paper primarily focuses on ℓp-norm bounded threat models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, including exploring new architectures and sampling techniques for diffusion models, and investigating different strategies for controlling randomness to enhance adversarial robustness.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel generative framework that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of data distributions residing on manifolds. LWGAN combines Wasserstein Auto-Encoders (WAE) and Wasserstein GANs (WGAN) to improve generative modeling and representation learning by identifying the intrinsic dimension of the data. The method includes theoretical guarantees for estimation consistency and generalization error bounds.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN utilizes a deterministic encoder to learn an informative latent distribution and a generator to produce realistic images. The framework modifies the latent distribution from N(0, Id) to a generalized normal distribution N(0, A), where A is a diagonal matrix allowing characterization of the latent space's intrinsic dimension. The training process involves a minimax optimization based on the 1-Wasserstein distance and includes a rank-regularized objective function.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework was validated on synthetic datasets (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image datasets (MNIST, CelebA). The performance was evaluated by comparing generated samples, reconstructed data, and interpolation results. Quantitative metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error were used to compare LWGAN against WGAN, WAE, and CycleGAN.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper assumes a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The Lipschitz constraint on the critic function is enforced using the gradient penalty technique, which requires careful tuning of the regularization level. Also, the computational cost of training d different sets of neural networks may be high.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general stochastic generator, incorporating LWGAN into more recent GAN modules like BigGAN to produce high-resolution images, and exploring applications in structural estimation for quantifying economic mechanisms and learning the effects of policies.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
A Diffusion Model Framework for Unsupervised Neural Combinatorial
  Optimization
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Learning to sample from intractable distributions over discrete sets without
relying on corresponding training data is a central problem in a wide range of
fields, including Combinatorial Optimization. Currently, popular deep
learning-based approaches rely primarily on generative models that yield exact
sample likelihoods. This work introduces a method that lifts this restriction
and opens the possibility to employ highly expressive latent variable models
like diffusion models. Our approach is conceptually based on a loss that upper
bounds the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence and evades the requirement of
exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for data-free unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. It addresses the challenge of sampling from intractable discrete distributions in combinatorial optimization by using an upper bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence as a loss function, enabling the use of latent variable models like diffusion models without requiring exact sample likelihoods. DiffUCO achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of benchmark problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DiffUCO employs a diffusion model to approximate the Boltzmann distribution associated with combinatorial optimization problems. The method involves a forward diffusion process that transforms the target distribution into a stationary distribution by iteratively adding noise, and a reverse process modeled by the diffusion model to map samples from the stationary distribution back to the target distribution. The model is trained by minimizing a Joint Variational Upper Bound on the reverse KL divergence. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are used to improve solution quality and efficiency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on five different CO problem types: Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC). Datasets include RB-model graphs for MIS and MaxCl, and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs for MDS and MaxCut. Performance is compared against state-of-the-art autoregressive models and classical solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include average solution size and approximation ratio.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The learned probability distribution will always have a bias, meaning the model samples do not exactly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity is memory- and time-expensive.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include improving memory and time efficiency by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation, exploring latent diffusion models, and using U-Net inspired GNN architectures. Another direction involves improving the method's ability to generate unbiased samples and enhancing out-of-distribution generalization.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
NExT-Mol: 3D Diffusion Meets 1D Language Modeling for 3D Molecule
  Generation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
3D molecule generation is crucial for drug discovery and material design.
While prior efforts focus on 3D diffusion models for their benefits in modeling
continuous 3D conformers, they overlook the advantages of 1D SELFIES-based
Language Models (LMs), which can generate 100% valid molecules and leverage the
billion-scale 1D molecule datasets. To combine these advantages for 3D molecule
generation, we propose a foundation model -- NExT-Mol: 3D Diffusion Meets 1D
Language Modeling for 3D Molecule Generation. NExT-Mol uses an extensively
pretrained molecule LM for 1D molecule generation, and subsequently predicts
the generated molecule's 3D conformers with a 3D diffusion model. We enhance
NExT-Mol's performance by scaling up the LM's model size, refining the
diffusion neural architecture, and applying 1D to 3D transfer learning.
Notably, our 1D molecule LM significantly outperforms baselines in
distributional similarity while ensuring validity, and our 3D diffusion model
achieves leading performances in conformer prediction. Given these improvements
in 1D and 3D modeling, NExT-Mol achieves a 26% relative improvement in 3D FCD
for de novo 3D generation on GEOM-DRUGS, and a 13% average relative gain for
conditional 3D generation on QM9-2014. Our codes and pretrained checkpoints are
available at https://github.com/acharkq/NExT-Mol.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces NExT-Mol, a foundation model for 3D molecule generation that combines 1D SELFIES-based Language Models (LMs) with 3D diffusion models to generate valid and accurate 3D molecular structures. Key contributions include scaling up molecular LMs, refining 3D diffusion model architectures, and applying 1D-to-3D transfer learning.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: NExT-Mol uses a pretrained Molecular Llama LM (MoLlama) for 1D molecule generation and a Diffusion Molecular Transformer (DMT) to predict 3D conformers. DMT incorporates Relational Multi-Head Self-Attention to retain 2D molecular graph information and uses adaptive layernorm. Transfer learning from 1D to 3D is achieved via a cross-modal projector.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The model was evaluated on GEOM-DRUGS, GEOM-QM9, and QM9-2014 datasets. Performance was measured using metrics like FCD, atom stability, bond length, bond angle, dihedral angle, COV-R, and AMR. Baselines included CDGS, JODO, MiDi, G-SchNet, G-SphereNet, EDM, MDM, GeoLDM, EEGSDE, EQGAT-diff, MolGPT, MolGen, OMEGA, GeoMol, GeoDiff, Torsional Diffusion, Particle Guidance and MCF.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Limitations include the computational cost of incorporating a large LM, the quadratic memory complexity of DMT's pair representation, and the generalization of conformer prediction to unseen scaffolds. The impact of randomized SELFIES augmentation in pretraining was not fully explored.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include exploring NExT-Mol for structure-based molecule generation, using bi-directional self-attention in the LM, improving generalization to unseen scaffolds, exploring advanced diffusion guidance methods, and building multi-modal foundation models for tasks like chemical reaction prediction and drug-drug interaction prediction.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Imitation learning addresses the challenge of learning by observing an
expert's demonstrations without access to reward signals from environments.
Most existing imitation learning methods that do not require interacting with
environments either model the expert distribution as the conditional
probability p(a|s) (e.g., behavioral cloning, BC) or the joint probability p(s,
a). Despite the simplicity of modeling the conditional probability with BC, it
usually struggles with generalization. While modeling the joint probability can
improve generalization performance, the inference procedure is often
time-consuming, and the model can suffer from manifold overfitting. This work
proposes an imitation learning framework that benefits from modeling both the
conditional and joint probability of the expert distribution. Our proposed
Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC) employs a diffusion model
trained to model expert behaviors and learns a policy to optimize both the BC
loss (conditional) and our proposed diffusion model loss (joint). DBC
outperforms baselines in various continuous control tasks in navigation, robot
arm manipulation, dexterous manipulation, and locomotion. We design additional
experiments to verify the limitations of modeling either the conditional
probability or the joint probability of the expert distribution, as well as
compare different generative models. Ablation studies justify the effectiveness
of our design choices.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC), a novel imitation learning framework that combines the efficiency of modeling the conditional probability p(a|s) (as in Behavioral Cloning) with the generalization ability of modeling the joint probability p(s, a) using a diffusion model. DBC outperforms existing methods in various continuous control tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DBC employs a diffusion model trained on expert demonstrations to capture the joint probability p(s, a) of state-action pairs. A policy is then trained to optimize both the standard Behavioral Cloning (BC) loss (conditional probability) and a proposed diffusion model loss, which leverages the diffusion model's estimation of how well a state-action pair fits the expert distribution. A normalization term is introduced to stabilize the diffusion model loss.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on continuous control tasks including navigation (MAZE), robot arm manipulation (FETCH PICK), dexterous manipulation (HAND ROTATE), and locomotion (CHEETAH, WALKER, ANTREACH). The performance was measured using success rate and return. Baselines included BC, Implicit BC, and Diffusion Policy. Generalization was tested by injecting noise into the initial state and goal locations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The framework is designed to learn from expert trajectories without interacting with the environment, meaning it cannot learn from agent trajectories. The method inherits biases from the expert demonstrations used for training. Also, the action sampling and optimization of Implicit BC may not scale well to high-dimensional action spaces.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes extending the method to incorporate agent data, which could improve performance when interacting with the environment is possible. Additionally, mitigating biases inherited from expert demonstrators is an important direction.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces a framework for training diffusion models using only corrupted samples, addressing an open problem in Ambient Diffusion. The key contributions include: (1) a method for learning optimal denoisers using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function to extend sampling below the observed data noise; (2) evidence that diffusion models memorize training sets, raising privacy concerns; (3) fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL with noisy samples to reduce memorization while maintaining performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves a double application of Tweedie's formula to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above the observed data noise. A consistency loss function is then used to learn denoisers for noise levels below the observed data noise. The method also includes a novel attack to demonstrate and quantify memorization in diffusion models by solving inverse problems with highly corrupted images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) on the LAION dataset and FFHQ dataset at 1024x1024 resolution. The models were fine-tuned on corrupted data with varying noise levels, and performance was evaluated using metrics such as FID score, MSE reconstruction error, and a similarity metric based on DINOv2 embeddings to quantify memorization. Training used the Adam optimizer with specific hyperparameters and LoRA.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not solve the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. The Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work on very limited datasets in preliminary experiments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include: (1) addressing the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data; (2) reducing the training time associated with consistency training; (3) exploring the applicability of the Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective on limited datasets.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: BTLM-3B-8K: 7B Parameter Performance in a 3B Parameter Model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce the Bittensor Language Model, called "BTLM-3B-8K", a new
state-of-the-art 3 billion parameter open-source language model. BTLM-3B-8K was
trained on 627B tokens from the SlimPajama dataset with a mixture of 2,048 and
8,192 context lengths. BTLM-3B-8K outperforms all existing 3B parameter models
by 2-5.5% across downstream tasks. BTLM-3B-8K is even competitive with some 7B
parameter models. Additionally, BTLM-3B-8K provides excellent long context
performance, outperforming MPT-7B-8K and XGen-7B-8K on tasks up to 8,192
context length. We trained the model on a cleaned and deduplicated SlimPajama
dataset; aggressively tuned the \textmu P hyperparameters and schedule; used
ALiBi position embeddings; and adopted the SwiGLU nonlinearity.
  On Hugging Face, the most popular models have 7B parameters, indicating that
users prefer the quality-size ratio of 7B models. Compacting the 7B parameter
model to one with 3B parameters, with little performance impact, is an
important milestone. BTLM-3B-8K needs only 3GB of memory with 4-bit precision
and takes 2.5x less inference compute than 7B models, helping to open up access
to a powerful language model on mobile and edge devices. BTLM-3B-8K is
available under an Apache 2.0 license on Hugging Face:
https://huggingface.co/cerebras/btlm-3b-8k-base.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces BTLM-3B-8K, a new 3 billion parameter open-source language model that achieves state-of-the-art performance among 3B parameter models and is competitive with some 7B parameter models, especially in long context tasks. The key contributions include the training procedure, model evaluation across various benchmarks, training improvement ablations, and the release of the BTLM-3B-8K weights and SlimPajama dataset.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: BTLM-3B-8K is an autoregressive transformer decoder model based on the GPT-3 architecture with fully dense attention. It incorporates SwiGLU nonlinearity, ALiBi position embeddings, and maximal update parameterization (µP). The model was trained in two phases with different sequence lengths, using the AdamW optimizer and a linear warmup and decay learning rate schedule.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The model was trained on the SlimPajama dataset, a cleaned and deduplicated version of RedPajama, containing 627B tokens. It was evaluated on 22 benchmarks, including common sense reasoning, world knowledge, reading comprehension, MMLU, code generation, and long context tasks. The evaluation used zero-shot and few-shot tasks from the EleutherAI evaluation harness framework. Long context performance was evaluated on QMSum, GovReports, and LongEval tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The model exhibits biases, toxicity, and truthfulness characteristics similar to existing models. ALiBi alone does not provide competitive extrapolation capability and the model learns to “overfit” to the current context length. BTLM-3B-8K underperforms every 7B model in average world knowledge accuracy. The model was only trained and evaluated following the approaches described in the paper.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests using variable context length training schedules to improve extrapolation performance. It recommends exploring harm mitigation strategies in deployment contexts and careful dataset curation techniques to reduce model harmfulness. Further research could focus on instruction-following or chat-based use cases. Additional safety-related testing and mitigations should be applied before using the model in production downstream applications.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Purify++, an improved diffusion purification algorithm for defending against adversarial attacks. It addresses limitations in previous diffusion purification methods by using an improved diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ utilizes an improved forward diffusion process based on Variance Exploding (VE) diffusion, efficient purification simulation using Heun's method, and optimal control of randomness by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics. The algorithm integrates the EDM diffusion model, Heun's simulation, and a randomness control mechanism.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The algorithm was evaluated on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets using various adversarial attacks, including black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive attacks (BPDA+EOT). Performance was measured using standard and robust accuracy, comparing against existing adversarial defense methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The evaluation of the adaptive attack (BPDA+EOT) was performed on a subset of the CIFAR10 test set due to high computational costs. The paper primarily focuses on ℓp-norm bounded threat models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, including exploring new architectures and sampling techniques for diffusion models, and investigating different strategies for controlling randomness to enhance adversarial robustness.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel generative framework that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of data distributions residing on manifolds. LWGAN combines Wasserstein Auto-Encoders (WAE) and Wasserstein GANs (WGAN) to improve generative modeling and representation learning by identifying the intrinsic dimension of the data. The method includes theoretical guarantees for estimation consistency and generalization error bounds.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN utilizes a deterministic encoder to learn an informative latent distribution and a generator to produce realistic images. The framework modifies the latent distribution from N(0, Id) to a generalized normal distribution N(0, A), where A is a diagonal matrix allowing characterization of the latent space's intrinsic dimension. The training process involves a minimax optimization based on the 1-Wasserstein distance and includes a rank-regularized objective function.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework was validated on synthetic datasets (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image datasets (MNIST, CelebA). The performance was evaluated by comparing generated samples, reconstructed data, and interpolation results. Quantitative metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error were used to compare LWGAN against WGAN, WAE, and CycleGAN.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper assumes a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The Lipschitz constraint on the critic function is enforced using the gradient penalty technique, which requires careful tuning of the regularization level. Also, the computational cost of training d different sets of neural networks may be high.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general stochastic generator, incorporating LWGAN into more recent GAN modules like BigGAN to produce high-resolution images, and exploring applications in structural estimation for quantifying economic mechanisms and learning the effects of policies.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
A Diffusion Model Framework for Unsupervised Neural Combinatorial
  Optimization
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Learning to sample from intractable distributions over discrete sets without
relying on corresponding training data is a central problem in a wide range of
fields, including Combinatorial Optimization. Currently, popular deep
learning-based approaches rely primarily on generative models that yield exact
sample likelihoods. This work introduces a method that lifts this restriction
and opens the possibility to employ highly expressive latent variable models
like diffusion models. Our approach is conceptually based on a loss that upper
bounds the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence and evades the requirement of
exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for data-free unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. It addresses the challenge of sampling from intractable discrete distributions in combinatorial optimization by using an upper bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence as a loss function, enabling the use of latent variable models like diffusion models without requiring exact sample likelihoods. DiffUCO achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of benchmark problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DiffUCO employs a diffusion model to approximate the Boltzmann distribution associated with combinatorial optimization problems. The method involves a forward diffusion process that transforms the target distribution into a stationary distribution by iteratively adding noise, and a reverse process modeled by the diffusion model to map samples from the stationary distribution back to the target distribution. The model is trained by minimizing a Joint Variational Upper Bound on the reverse KL divergence. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are used to improve solution quality and efficiency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on five different CO problem types: Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC). Datasets include RB-model graphs for MIS and MaxCl, and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs for MDS and MaxCut. Performance is compared against state-of-the-art autoregressive models and classical solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include average solution size and approximation ratio.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The learned probability distribution will always have a bias, meaning the model samples do not exactly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity is memory- and time-expensive.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include improving memory and time efficiency by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation, exploring latent diffusion models, and using U-Net inspired GNN architectures. Another direction involves improving the method's ability to generate unbiased samples and enhancing out-of-distribution generalization.

A consistency loss function is then used to learn denoisers for noise levels below the observed data noise. The method also includes a novel attack to demonstrate and quantify memorization in diffusion models by solving inverse problems with highly corrupted images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) on the LAION dataset and FFHQ dataset at 1024x1024 resolution. The models were fine-tuned on corrupted data with varying noise levels, and performance was evaluated using metrics such as FID score, MSE reconstruction error, and a similarity metric based on DINOv2 embeddings to quantify memorization. Training used the Adam optimizer with specific hyperparameters and LoRA.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not solve the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. The Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work on very limited datasets in preliminary experiments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include: (1) addressing the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data; (2) reducing the training time associated with consistency training; (3) exploring the applicability of the Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective on limited datasets.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Purify++, an improved diffusion purification algorithm for defending against adversarial attacks. It addresses limitations in previous diffusion purification methods by using an improved diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: Purify++ utilizes an improved forward diffusion process based on Variance Exploding (VE) diffusion, efficient purification simulation using Heun's method, and optimal control of randomness by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics. The algorithm integrates the EDM diffusion model, Heun's simulation, and a randomness control mechanism.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The algorithm was evaluated on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets using various adversarial attacks, including black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive attacks (BPDA+EOT). Performance was measured using standard and robust accuracy, comparing against existing adversarial defense methods.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The evaluation of the adaptive attack (BPDA+EOT) was performed on a subset of the CIFAR10 test set due to high computational costs. The paper primarily focuses on ℓp-norm bounded threat models.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, including exploring new architectures and sampling techniques for diffusion models, and investigating different strategies for controlling randomness to enhance adversarial robustness.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
A Diffusion Model Framework for Unsupervised Neural Combinatorial
  Optimization
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Learning to sample from intractable distributions over discrete sets without
relying on corresponding training data is a central problem in a wide range of
fields, including Combinatorial Optimization. Currently, popular deep
learning-based approaches rely primarily on generative models that yield exact
sample likelihoods. This work introduces a method that lifts this restriction
and opens the possibility to employ highly expressive latent variable models
like diffusion models. Our approach is conceptually based on a loss that upper
bounds the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence and evades the requirement of
exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for data-free unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. It addresses the challenge of sampling from intractable discrete distributions in combinatorial optimization by using an upper bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence as a loss function, enabling the use of latent variable models like diffusion models without requiring exact sample likelihoods. DiffUCO achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of benchmark problems.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: DiffUCO employs a diffusion model to approximate the Boltzmann distribution associated with combinatorial optimization problems. The method involves a forward diffusion process that transforms the target distribution into a stationary distribution by iteratively adding noise, and a reverse process modeled by the diffusion model to map samples from the stationary distribution back to the target distribution. The model is trained by minimizing a Joint Variational Upper Bound on the reverse KL divergence. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are used to improve solution quality and efficiency.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on five different CO problem types: Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC). Datasets include RB-model graphs for MIS and MaxCl, and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs for MDS and MaxCut. Performance is compared against state-of-the-art autoregressive models and classical solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include average solution size and approximation ratio.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The learned probability distribution will always have a bias, meaning the model samples do not exactly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity is memory- and time-expensive.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include improving memory and time efficiency by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation, exploring latent diffusion models, and using U-Net inspired GNN architectures. Another direction involves improving the method's ability to generate unbiased samples and enhancing out-of-distribution generalization.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Imitation learning addresses the challenge of learning by observing an
expert's demonstrations without access to reward signals from environments.
Most existing imitation learning methods that do not require interacting with
environments either model the expert distribution as the conditional
probability p(a|s) (e.g., behavioral cloning, BC) or the joint probability p(s,
a). Despite the simplicity of modeling the conditional probability with BC, it
usually struggles with generalization. While modeling the joint probability can
improve generalization performance, the inference procedure is often
time-consuming, and the model can suffer from manifold overfitting. This work
proposes an imitation learning framework that benefits from modeling both the
conditional and joint probability of the expert distribution. Our proposed
Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC) employs a diffusion model
trained to model expert behaviors and learns a policy to optimize both the BC
loss (conditional) and our proposed diffusion model loss (joint). DBC
outperforms baselines in various continuous control tasks in navigation, robot
arm manipulation, dexterous manipulation, and locomotion. We design additional
experiments to verify the limitations of modeling either the conditional
probability or the joint probability of the expert distribution, as well as
compare different generative models. Ablation studies justify the effectiveness
of our design choices.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC), a novel imitation learning framework that combines the efficiency of modeling the conditional probability p(a|s) (as in Behavioral Cloning) with the generalization ability of modeling the joint probability p(s, a) using a diffusion model. DBC outperforms existing methods in various continuous control tasks.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: DBC employs a diffusion model trained on expert demonstrations to capture the joint probability p(s, a) of state-action pairs. A policy is then trained to optimize both the standard Behavioral Cloning (BC) loss (conditional probability) and a proposed diffusion model loss, which leverages the diffusion model's estimation of how well a state-action pair fits the expert distribution. A normalization term is introduced to stabilize the diffusion model loss.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on continuous control tasks including navigation (MAZE), robot arm manipulation (FETCH PICK), dexterous manipulation (HAND ROTATE), and locomotion (CHEETAH, WALKER, ANTREACH). The performance was measured using success rate and return. Baselines included BC, Implicit BC, and Diffusion Policy. Generalization was tested by injecting noise into the initial state and goal locations.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The framework is designed to learn from expert trajectories without interacting with the environment, meaning it cannot learn from agent trajectories. The method inherits biases from the expert demonstrations used for training. Also, the action sampling and optimization of Implicit BC may not scale well to high-dimensional action spaces.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes extending the method to incorporate agent data, which could improve performance when interacting with the environment is possible. Additionally, mitigating biases inherited from expert demonstrators is an important direction.

base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2409.18374v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2409.18374v1","title":"Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks","authors":["Yixuan Qiu","Qingyi Gao","Xiao Wang"],"published_date":"2024-09-27T01:25:22Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.","github_url":"https://github.com/yixuan/LWGAN","main_contributions":"The paper introduces the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel framework that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds. LWGAN combines Wasserstein Auto-Encoders (WAE) and Wasserstein GANs (WGAN) to improve generative modeling and representation learning by identifying the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold. It theoretically proves the existence of an encoder and generator network where the learned encoding distribution's intrinsic dimension matches the data manifold's dimension and establishes the consistency of the estimated intrinsic dimension and provides an upper bound on the generalization error.","methodology":"LWGAN uses a modified latent distribution, specifically a generalized normal distribution N(0, A) where A is a diagonal matrix with 0 or 1 entries, to characterize the intrinsic dimension of the latent space. It combines a deterministic encoder Q: X -> Z to learn an informative prior distribution PZ ~ N(0, A) with a generator G: Z -> X to generate realistic images. The framework minimizes the 1-Wasserstein distance between the real and generated data distributions, regularized by a rank penalty on matrix A.","experimental_setup":"The framework was validated using synthetic datasets (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image datasets (MNIST, CelebA). The performance was evaluated based on the accuracy of intrinsic dimension detection, visual quality of generated samples, and quantitative metrics like Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error. Baselines included WGAN, WAE, and CycleGAN.","limitations":"The paper assumes the existence of a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The generator G is assumed to be continuous. The 1-Lipschitz constraint on the critic f is enforced by the gradient penalty technique.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions include investigating a more general scenario where the generator G is stochastic by adding an extra noise vector to its input. It also suggests incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules such as BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images along with the estimation of the intrinsic dimension. Applying LWGAN for structural estimation in economics is also proposed."}
